start.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
start.text: Draven is seated at the inn table, a map of the region spread before him. He's marked Ashvale with a black circle. Red arrows converge on it from three directions.

start_2.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
start_2.text: You've come to talk terms. Good. I'd rather sign a treaty than write a casualty report. Sit. Let's be civilized about the end of your independence.

options.text: Trade routes. That's what the Legion really wants — the Thornwood road. Let's start there.
No garrison. Ashvale keeps its own guard. We'll permit Legion patrols on the road but not in the village.
Before we negotiate — the spy you mentioned. I want to know more.

trade_discussion.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
trade_discussion.text: The Thornwood road saves our supply lines three weeks per circuit. That's worth more than this village, frankly. You're sharp to see it.

trade_discussion_2.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
trade_discussion_2.text: Here's what I can offer: guaranteed trade access, protected by Legion outriders. Ashvale becomes a waystation — inn, stables, resupply point. Your merchants get first access to eastern goods. In exchange: a percentage of all goods passing through. Say... fifteen percent.

options.text: Fifteen percent is steep, but manageable. What about the conscription quotas?
Ten percent. And Ashvale sets the tariffs on goods sold within village limits.

counter_response.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
counter_response.text: Draven taps the map twice. He's calculating.

counter_response_2.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
counter_response_2.text: Twelve percent. Local tariff authority. But the Legion reserves the right to audit your books once per season. Non-negotiable — the General doesn't trust anyone's arithmetic but his own.

conscription_discussion.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
conscription_discussion.text: Now — conscription. The standard quota is one in ten fighting-age adults per year. For a village this size, that's... perhaps eight to twelve souls. They serve for three years, then return home.

options.text: No conscription. Ashvale's people are farmers, not soldiers. You want troops, recruit volunteers and pay them.
One in ten is harsh, but... if it keeps the peace, it keeps the peace.
Volunteer-only. The Legion pays a fair wage. Anyone who wants to serve can serve — but no one is forced.

conscription_refused.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
conscription_refused.text: Draven's jaw tightens. This is where the mask gets thin.

conscription_refused_2.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
conscription_refused_2.text: The General will not accept a deal without manpower provisions. Period. I can reduce the quota — one in twenty, perhaps. But zero is a number that ends this negotiation.

conscription_counter.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
conscription_counter.text: He considers this for a long moment, rolling a coin across his knuckles.

conscription_counter_2.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
conscription_counter_2.text: Volunteer recruitment with a signing bonus paid by the Legion. Minimum of five volunteers per year or Ashvale covers the shortfall in gold. That... I can sell to the General. Barely.

deal_summary.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
deal_summary.text: Draven pulls a blank parchment from his satchel and begins writing in precise, small letters.

deal_summary_2.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
deal_summary_2.text: Trade access, tribute in goods, local governance retained under Legion oversight. Your lord keeps his seat. I'll present this to the General. If he agrees — and I believe he will — Ashvale becomes a tributary partner, not a conquered province.

deal_summary_3.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
deal_summary_3.text: He sets down the quill and looks at you directly.

deal_summary_4.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
deal_summary_4.text: You negotiated well. Better than most. But let me be clear about one thing: the Legion keeps its word, and it expects the same. Default on these terms and there won't be a second conversation. There will only be the sound of boots on your road.

spy_discussion_2.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
spy_discussion_2.text: I told you what I told you because it serves the negotiation. A frightened partner is a compliant partner. But I won't burn my sources.

spy_discussion_3.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
spy_discussion_3.text: What I will say: the reports started three months ago. Shortly after a certain priest arrived in your village. Make of that what you will.

spy_discussion_4.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
spy_discussion_4.text: Now. Can we return to the matter of your village's future? Or would you prefer to chase shadows while the clock ticks?

military_discussion.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
military_discussion.text: No garrison. That's your opening position. Here's mine: a garrison is the only thing that makes this deal work for the General. Without soldiers on the ground, this is just a piece of paper.

military_discussion_2.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
military_discussion_2.text: Compromise: a waystation barracks on the road outside the village, not within it. Twenty soldiers, rotated quarterly. They patrol the road, not the village. Your guard handles internal matters. The Legion handles external threats.

options.text: A barracks outside the village. That... could work. But the village guard answers to Lord Ashworth, not the Legion.
No soldiers. Not in the village, not outside it. The road is open to merchants and travelers. That should be enough.

military_hardline.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
military_hardline.text: Draven stands abruptly, rolling up the map.

military_hardline_2.speaker: ironmarch_emissary
military_hardline_2.text: Then we have nothing to discuss. The Legion doesn't invest in unprotected assets. You have your seven days. Use them to prepare for what comes next, because what comes next won't be sitting at a table with maps.
